is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") where [input](/wiki/Input_device "Input device") from only one player is expected throughout the [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay"). Video games in general can feature several [game modes](/wiki/Game_mode "Game mode"), including single-player modes designed to be played by a single player in addition to [multi-player](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game") modes.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Video game that permits only one player

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ab/Frozen-bubble-screenshot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frozen-bubble-screenshot.jpg)

A screenshot of the game *[Frozen Bubble](/wiki/Frozen_Bubble "Frozen Bubble")*

A **single-player video game** is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") where [input](/wiki/Input_device "Input device") from only one player is expected throughout the [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay"). Video games in general can feature several [game modes](/wiki/Game_mode "Game mode"), including single-player modes designed to be played by a single player in addition to [multi-player](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game") modes.

Most modern [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game"), [PC games](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") and [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") are designed so that they can be played by a single player; although many of these games have modes that allow two or more players to play (not necessarily simultaneously), very few actually require more than one player for the game to be played. The *[Unreal Tournament](/wiki/Unreal_Tournament "Unreal Tournament")* series is one example of such.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The earliest video games, such as *[Tennis for Two](/wiki/Tennis_for_Two "Tennis for Two")* (1958), *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* (1962), and *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")* (1972), were symmetrical games designed to be played by two players. Single-player games gained popularity only after this, with early titles such as *[Speed Race](/wiki/Speed_Race "Speed Race")* (1974)[[2]](#cite_note-2) and *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* (1978).

The reason for this, according to [Raph Koster](/wiki/Raph_Koster "Raph Koster"), is down to a combination of several factors: increasingly sophisticated computers and interfaces that enabled [asymmetric](/wiki/Asymmetric_warfare "Asymmetric warfare") gameplay, [cooperative gameplay](/wiki/Cooperative_video_game "Cooperative video game") and story delivery within a gaming framework, coupled with the fact that the majority of early games players had introverted personality types (according to the [Myers-Briggs personality type indicator](/wiki/Myers-Briggs_Type_Indicator "Myers-Briggs Type Indicator")).[[3]](#cite_note-RaphKoster-3)

Although most modern games incorporate a single-player element either as the core or as one of several game modes, single-player gaming had been viewed by the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry") as peripheral to the future of gaming, with [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") vice president Frank Gibeau stating in 2012 that he had not approved one game to be developed as a single-player experience.[[4]](#cite_note-CVG-4)

The question of the financial viability of single-player [AAA](/wiki/AAA_(video_gaming) "AAA (video gaming)") games was raised following the closure of [Visceral Games](/wiki/Visceral_Games "Visceral Games") by [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") (EA) in October 2017. Visceral had been a studio that established itself on a strong narrative single-player focus with *[Dead Space](/wiki/Dead_Space_(series) "Dead Space (series)")*, and had been working on a single-player, linear narrative *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* game at the time of the closure; EA announced following this that they would be taking the game in a different direction, specifically "a broader experience that allows for more variety and player agency".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Many commentators felt that EA made the change as they did not have confidence that a studio with an AAA-scale budget could produce a viable single-player game based on the popular *Star Wars* franchise. Alongside this, as well as relatively poor sales of games in the year prior that were principally AAA single-player games (*[Resident Evil 7](/wiki/Resident_Evil_7 "Resident Evil 7")*, *[Prey](/wiki/Prey_(2017_video_game) "Prey (2017 video game)")*, *[Dishonored 2](/wiki/Dishonored_2 "Dishonored 2")*, and *[Deus Ex: Mankind Divided](/wiki/Deus_Ex:_Mankind_Divided "Deus Ex: Mankind Divided")*) against financially successful multiplayer games and those offer a games-as-a-service model (*[Overwatch](/wiki/Overwatch_(video_game) "Overwatch (video game)")*, *[Destiny 2](/wiki/Destiny_2 "Destiny 2")*, and *[Star Wars Battlefront 2](/wiki/Star_Wars_Battlefront_II_(2017_video_game) "Star Wars Battlefront II (2017 video game)")*), were indicators to many that the single-player model for AAA was waning.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-usgamer_history-9) Manveer Heir, who had left EA after finishing his gameplay design work for *[Mass Effect Andromeda](/wiki/Mass_Effect_Andromeda "Mass Effect Andromeda")*, acknowledged that the culture within EA was against the development of single-player games, and with Visceral's closure, "that the linear single-player triple-A game at EA is dead for the time being".[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Bethesda](/wiki/Bethesda_Softworks "Bethesda Softworks") on December 7, 2017, decided to collaborate with [Lynda Carter](/wiki/Lynda_Carter "Lynda Carter") to launch a Public Safety Announcement to save single-player gaming.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A few years later in 2021, EA was reported to have revived interest in single-player games, following the successful launch of *[Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order](/wiki/Star_Wars_Jedi:_Fallen_Order "Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order")* in 2020. The company still planned on releasing live service games with multiplayer components, but began evaluating its IP catalog for more single-player titles to revive, such as a remake of the *[Dead Space](/wiki/Dead_Space_(franchise) "Dead Space (franchise)")* franchise.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Around the same time, head of [Xbox Game Studios](/wiki/Xbox_Game_Studios "Xbox Game Studios") Phil Spencer said that they still see a place for narrative-driven single-player games even though the financial drivers of the market tended to be live service games. Spencer said that developing such games with AAA-scale budgets can be risky, but with availability of services like [cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming") and subscription services, they can gauge audience reaction to these games early on and reduce the risk involved before releases.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Game elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Single-player_video_game&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Game elements")]

As the narrative and conflict in single-player gameplay is created by a computer rather than a human opponent, single-player games are able to deliver certain gaming experiences that are typically absent—or de-emphasised—in multiplayer games.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Story

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Single-player_video_game&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Story")]

See also: [Narrative of video games](/wiki/Narrative_of_video_games "Narrative of video games")

Single-player games rely more heavily on compelling stories to draw the player into the experience and to create a sense of investment. Humans are unpredictable, so human players - allies or enemies - cannot be relied upon to carry a narrative in a particular direction, and so multiplayer games tend not to focus heavily on a linear narrative. By contrast, many single-player games are built around a compelling story.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Single-player_video_game&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Characters")]

While a multiplayer game relies upon human-human interaction for its conflict, and often for its sense of camaraderie, a single-player game must build these things artificially. As such, single-player games require deeper characterisation of their [non-player characters](/wiki/Non-player_character "Non-player character") in order to create connections between the player and the sympathetic characters and to develop deeper antipathy towards the game's [antagonists](/wiki/Antagonist "Antagonist"). This is typically true of [role-playing games](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") (RPGs), such as *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest")* and the *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*, which are primarily character-driven and have a different setting.

### Exceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Single-player_video_game&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Exceptions")]

These game elements are not firm, fixed rules; single-player [puzzle games](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") such as *[Tetris](/wiki/Tetris "Tetris")* or [racing games](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") focus squarely on gameplay.
